nodes.name: Physics
nodes.description: Laws, forces, and the fundamental nature of matter and energy

items.prompt: What is the approximate speed of light in a vacuum, in kilometers per second?
items.explanation:
Exactly 299,792,458 m/s — the metre is now defined so that c is this exact value
Denoted c (from Latin celeritas, 'speed'); a fundamental constant of the universe
Special relativity (Einstein, 1905): nothing with mass can reach or exceed c
Light from the Sun takes ~8 minutes 20 seconds to reach Earth; from the Moon, ~1.3 seconds
A light-year is ~9.46 × 10¹⁵ m; the nearest star (Proxima Centauri) is ~4.2 light-years away

items.prompt: What is Newton's First Law of Motion, also known by what common name?
alternates: objects at rest stay at rest and objects in motion stay in motion unless acted on by a net force
items.explanation:
Formulated by Newton in *Principia Mathematica* (1687), building on earlier work by Galileo
Inertia: the tendency of an object to resist changes to its state of motion; proportional to its mass
Explains why passengers lurch forward when a car brakes — their bodies tend to continue moving
Explains why planets keep orbiting: no friction in space means no force to slow them down
Aristotle incorrectly believed a constant force was needed to maintain motion; Galileo overturned this

items.prompt: What is Newton's Second Law of Motion, expressing the relationship between force, mass, and acceleration as an equation?
items.explanation:
Net force equals mass times acceleration: F = ma
Doubling the force on an object doubles its acceleration; doubling the mass halves the acceleration
Explains why heavy and light objects fall at the same rate in a vacuum: gravity exerts more force on heavier objects in exact proportion to their greater inertia
In SI units: force is measured in Newtons (N); 1 N = 1 kg·m/s²
Also written as F = Δp/Δt (rate of change of momentum), which is how Newton originally stated it

items.prompt: What is Newton's Third Law of Motion, governing action-reaction force pairs?
items.answer: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction
items.explanation:
When object A exerts a force on object B, B exerts an equal and opposite force on A
The two forces act on different objects — they are never the same force and do not cancel each other
Explains rocket propulsion: exhaust gases pushed backward propel the rocket forward
Explains walking: you push backward on the ground; the ground pushes you forward
Explains why firing a gun causes recoil: the bullet is pushed forward; the gun is pushed backward

links.text: For every action there is an equal and opposite reaction

items.prompt: What equation, published by Einstein in 1905, expresses the equivalence of mass and energy?
items.explanation:
E = mc²: energy equals mass times the speed of light squared
Published in Einstein's 1905 paper 'Does the Inertia of a Body Depend Upon Its Energy Content?'
Small amounts of mass contain enormous energy: 1 kg ≈ 9×10¹⁶ joules (~21 megatons of TNT)
Basis of nuclear energy: the mass defect in fission and fusion is the source of the released energy
The Sun converts ~4 million tons of mass to energy per second via nuclear fusion

items.prompt: What law, named for a German physicist, states that current through a conductor is directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance?
items.explanation:
V = IR: voltage (V) = current (I) × resistance (R)
Formulated by Georg Simon Ohm in 1827; published in *Die galvanische Kette, mathematisch bearbeitet*
The ohm (Ω) is the SI unit of electrical resistance, named after him
Ohm's Law holds for 'ohmic' (resistive) materials; it breaks down for semiconductors, diodes, and plasma
Fundamental to all electrical circuit design: doubling voltage doubles current if resistance is constant

items.prompt: What principle, attributed to the ancient Greek mathematician of Syracuse, states that the buoyant force on a submerged object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces?
items.explanation:
Formulated by Archimedes of Syracuse (~287–212 BC) in his treatise *On Floating Bodies*
Legend: Archimedes discovered it while stepping into a bath and seeing water overflow, then ran through Syracuse shouting 'Eureka!'
Explains why steel ships float: the hull displaces water weighing more than the ship itself
Explains why it's easier to float in the Dead Sea: dense saltwater exerts more buoyant force per unit volume
He also used it to detect fraud: showed a king's crown was not pure gold by comparing its weight in air vs. submerged in water
items.cardFront: Buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced

links.text: Buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced

items.prompt: What law of thermodynamics states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted between forms — also called the law of conservation of energy?
items.explanation:
ΔU = Q − W: change in internal energy equals heat added minus work done by the system
Energy can be converted between kinetic, potential, thermal, chemical, and nuclear forms, but total energy is constant
Formulated independently by Julius Robert von Mayer, James Prescott Joule, and Hermann von Helmholtz in the 1840s
Explains why a perpetual motion machine of the first kind (creates energy from nothing) is impossible
The calorie and joule are units of energy; James Joule demonstrated the mechanical equivalent of heat
items.cardFront: Energy cannot be created or destroyed

links.text: Energy cannot be created or destroyed

items.prompt: What law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of an isolated system always increases over time, and that heat flows spontaneously only from hot to cold?
items.explanation:
Entropy (S): a measure of disorder, or the number of possible microstates of a system
The universe always moves toward greater entropy (disorder) over time — you can't unscramble an egg
Rudolf Clausius (1850) and Lord Kelvin formulated early versions; Boltzmann gave the statistical interpretation (S = k ln W)
Explains the 'arrow of time': physical laws are symmetric in time, but entropy's increase gives time its direction
A perpetual motion machine of the second kind (converts all heat to work) is impossible under this law

items.prompt: What principle in quantum mechanics, formulated in 1927, states that the more precisely a particle's position is known, the less precisely its momentum can be known, and vice versa?
items.explanation:
Formulated by Werner Heisenberg in 1927: ΔxΔp ≥ ħ/2
Not a limitation of measurement technology — a fundamental property of quantum systems
Arises from wave-particle duality: waves cannot have both a precise position and a precise frequency simultaneously
Also applies to energy and time: ΔEΔt ≥ ħ/2 (explains the natural linewidth of atomic spectral lines)
Explains why electrons don't spiral into the nucleus: they can't have both zero momentum and zero distance from the nucleus
items.cardFront: Position and momentum cannot both be precisely known

links.text: Position and momentum cannot both be precisely known

items.prompt: What gas law, published in 1662, states that the pressure of a fixed amount of gas at constant temperature is inversely proportional to its volume?
items.explanation:
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ at constant temperature and constant amount of gas
Published by Robert Boyle in 1662; one of the earliest quantitative laws in science
Explains why gas in a syringe compresses when you push the plunger: pressure increases as volume decreases
A component of the ideal gas law (PV = nRT), combined with Charles's Law and Avogadro's Law
Practical application: scuba diving — dissolved nitrogen expands dangerously on ascent if pressure decreases too fast, causing decompression sickness ('the bends')

items.prompt: What effect, named for an Austrian physicist, describes the apparent change in frequency of a wave as its source and an observer move relative to each other?
items.explanation:
Proposed by Christian Doppler in 1842; confirmed experimentally for sound waves and later for light
Moving toward source: waves compressed → higher frequency (higher pitch; blue-shifted light)
Moving away from source: waves stretched → lower frequency (lower pitch; red-shifted light)
Classic example: ambulance siren pitch drops as it passes
Edwin Hubble used redshift (Doppler shift of light from galaxies) to establish that the universe is expanding
Police radar guns, weather radar, and medical Doppler ultrasound all use this effect
items.cardFront: Frequency change due to relative motion

links.text: Frequency change due to relative motion

items.prompt: What 1935 thought experiment, devised by an Austrian physicist, places a cat in a sealed box with a radioactive trigger to illustrate the paradox of quantum superposition?
items.explanation:
Devised by Erwin Schrödinger in 1935 as a critique of the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics
Setup: cat in sealed box with radioactive atom, detector, and poison vial — the cat is 'simultaneously alive and dead' until observed
Designed to expose the absurdity of applying quantum superposition to macroscopic objects
The 'measurement problem': at what scale does quantum superposition give way to classical reality?
The many-worlds interpretation avoids the collapse: both outcomes occur in separate branches of reality
Became a cultural icon for 'existing in two states at once'
items.cardFront: Cat in a box; quantum superposition paradox

links.text: Cat in a box; quantum superposition paradox

items.prompt: What principle, formulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1925, states that no two identical fermions in a quantum system can occupy the same quantum state simultaneously?
items.explanation:
Formulated by Wolfgang Pauli in 1925; he received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1945
Applies to fermions (particles with half-integer spin: electrons, protons, neutrons)
Explains atomic structure: each electron in an atom must have a unique combination of quantum numbers
Explains why matter is solid: electrons in atoms resist occupying the same states, preventing atoms from passing through each other
Neutron stars are supported against gravitational collapse by neutron degeneracy pressure — a direct consequence of this principle
items.cardFront: No two identical fermions share a quantum state

links.text: No two identical fermions share a quantum state

items.prompt: What observation, formulated by Edwin Hubble in 1929, showed that galaxies are receding from us at velocities proportional to their distance — evidence the universe is expanding?
items.explanation:
v = H₀d: recession velocity of a galaxy equals the Hubble constant times its distance
Hubble measured galactic distances with Cepheid variable stars and recession speeds via redshift
The current best estimate of H₀ is ~70 km/s per megaparsec (with ongoing debate between measurement methods)
Implies the universe had a beginning: extrapolating backward leads to the Big Bang ~13.8 billion years ago
Formally renamed the 'Hubble–Lemaître Law' in 2018 to credit Belgian priest Georges Lemaître, who derived it theoretically in 1927
